topics.title: Howard Stern's Legal Dispute with CBS Radio
topics.summary: The show features a discussion regarding the legal fallout of a lawsuit filed by CBS against Howard Stern, characterizing the litigation as a poorly planned and arrogant move by executive Les Moonves.

topics.title: Howard Stern's Legal Battle with CBS
topics.summary: The studio discusses the merits of a lawsuit filed by CBS against Howard Stern, characterizing it as a vindictive and frivolous legal action following his move to Sirius.

topics.title: CBS Lawsuit Against Howard Stern
topics.summary: The show features a discussion regarding the legal merits and motivations behind CBS's lawsuit against Howard Stern following his move to Sirius.

topics.title: Legal Battle and Industry Rivalries
topics.summary: The studio discusses a lawsuit involving CBS, the impact of legal costs on media figures, and the declining ratings of rival radio programs.

topics.title: Legal Battles and Media Commentary
topics.summary: The studio discusses the legal challenges facing Howard Stern, the career of Bubba the Love Sponge, a historical look at an AIDS-branded weight loss product, and upcoming segments featuring celebrity news and sports figures.

topics.title: Radio Industry Ratings and Legal Disputes
topics.summary: The studio discusses falling ratings for rival stations, a legal battle involving CBS Radio, and various segments featuring guest appearances and listener feedback.

topics.title: Legal Battles and Show Feedback
topics.summary: The studio reviews listener feedback on "The Better Half Show," discusses a lawsuit with CBS, and addresses the production of upcoming segments.

topics.title: Mr. Skin's Seventh Annual Anatomy Awards
topics.summary: The studio hosts a segment where Mr. Skin presents his annual film and television awards for categories such as best see-through top, full frontal scene, and best bikini.

topics.title: Film Nudity and Media Commentary
topics.summary: The studio discusses various films featuring nudity, the career of Mr. Skin, and a segment involving a guest with an upcoming statement.

topics.title: a Woman's Death
topics.summary: The studio features a heated exchange regarding personal grievances and the legal or social consequences surrounding the death of a woman.

topics.title: CBS Lawsuit Against Howard Stern
topics.summary: The studio discusses the legal battle between CBS and Howard Stern, characterizing the lawsuit as a vindictive move by executive Les Moonves that has inadvertently generated significant publicity for Stern's brand.

topics.title: Legal Disputes and Industry Dynamics
topics.summary: The show explores the complexities of a lawsuit involving punitive damages, the transition of radio programming to satellite, and the personal dynamics between media executives and talent.

topics.title: Radio Show Segment Previews
topics.summary: The studio prepares for upcoming segments featuring celebrity gossip, legal updates regarding Bubba the Love Sponge, and a discussion on Elliot Offen's recent appearances.

topics.title: Entertainment and Political News Segment
topics.summary: The show covers a variety of topics including the Eddie Murphy divorce, celebrity news involving Little Kim and Reese Witherspoon, reports on the Iraq War and presidential travel, and local advertisements.

transcript.text: What is it?
It's you.
Ah, good morning.
Where do I begin?
Have I gotten much sleep?
No.
I'll be honest with you.
I'm all charged up for my lawsuit, you know?
Want to hear my thoughts?
Go ahead.
Yeah.
You know, this is only day one after my press conference
and all hell is breaking loose.
First of all, I want to thank everyone for understanding what this lawsuit is.
The wave of news reports that are coming out,
Fox News, CNN, the MSNBC, all of them.
CBS covering?
CBS, who knows?
And in part of my voice today, it's going to take me a while to get warmed up.
I've been very charged up and very, very invigorated.
When I sat with Les Moonves, I said to him, "What are you doing?
You're going to be the laughingstock of the industry."
This is without merit.
It shows short-sightedness.
The articles that people are writing saying that this is a personal vendetta
is absolutely right.
It is a radio division without a plan.
Les Moonves, in my opinion, does not know what to do with the radio division.
He never wants to be involved with the radio division.
He's a television program director.
That's it.
And isn't this a distraction, both in the sense that he needs to be working on the radio division
and instead he's concentrating on this?
Yeah.
I said to him, "Here's a phone call. It's distracting me."
He says, "Please don't talk about your lawsuit today."
"I'll fine. Done deal. Over."
I had a lot of tape to play you and let you know what's going on.
Who is that person?
I don't know. I hear that stuff and it makes me mental.
What do you want me to talk about today, Nick?
Well, just not about the issue, I guess, because...
No, not about the issue.
If you go to your Howard News, which, you know, I don't have a problem doing and listening. That's fine.
A lot has happened since yesterday, but you know what? Maybe you're right. Let's...
Maybe not as long. I mean...
Well, it's going to be... Sometime I was going to play you a dizzying array of newscasts and...
I was going to take you around the world day one of this lawsuit.
I thought it was going to be very, very interesting, but...
Well, I thought an hour yesterday was a little bit more...
Well, okay. Well, there you go. I'll drop it. Let's move on.
I appreciate your call.
Thank you. Nick, you have what you want. I'm shutting up.
You appreciate the call. We don't...
No. No one wants to know what's going on.
No, no, no. Nick doesn't want to know.
Now, here's another guess. There's once an update on the lawsuit, so I don't know who to believe.
But I'll tell you the truth. Maybe I'll just go on with my instincts and...
Yeah.
If it's boring, tune over to...
Tell them to go somewhere else. I'm interested.
What are the choices around in the morning?
I got a feeling Nick is going to keep listening.
Yeah. You know what?
Nick hasn't entertained one person ever.
This lawsuit was started and was dreamt up in my opinion. This is just opinion. I'm guessing.
It's a pretty educated guess. The Joel Hollander, who has been in charge of the radio division since Mel Carmuson left,
is a very embarrassed man.
And him and a certain attorney that works there, they've had it in for me.
This attorney has it in for me. And they sat there and they go, "You know what? Let's sue Howard Stern."
And they brought this to Les Moonves. He's like George Bush, Les Moonves to me.
You know, he sits there and he holds court with his jesters, the three stooges.
They all get together in a room. And Les is a bully. Make no mistake about him.
And I'm finding out just how much of a bully he is. And this is a bully and it's a bully mentality.
And he sat there and they said, "Listen, let's go sue Howard Stern."
And the bully says, "Really, what is the definition of a bully?"
The definition of a bully is a guy who picks out a weak member and tortures him.
Tortures, tortures, tortures him because they want to feel like big men.
So Les Moonves sat in a room and said, "You know, it's not going to cost me a dime.
After all, I don't have to shut out my personal money. It's not going to cost me a dime to sue Howard Stern.
And what's the downside? There is no downside. Maybe I'll get something. Maybe I'll get something from him.
I won't be so embarrassed that the radio division is doing badly."
Or whatever. Who cares? He's a bully. He goes, "You know what?
A bully takes pleasure in other people's misery. Maybe Howard will lose sleep.
Hey, it'll distract him. He's doing a great job for Sirius over there. Don Buchwald."
He's enjoying himself.
"I'll call Don Buchwald a crook and a fraud. And you know what? Maybe it'll give him an ulcer.
Maybe it'll make him sick. He's an older guy.
Despite the fact that Don worked there for 14 years putting together a syndication effort for them
that made them millions and millions of dollars, a syndication of my show and orchestrated all of that.
Despite all that, screw loyalty, I'm a bully and I love to give other people pain.
And I'm sitting here on top of the world. The CBS television program is the number one.
He forgets he's running the rest of the division that's all going in the toilet."
Right. All he can see is TV.
And he goes, "Eh, you know what? Let's have some fun. Let's have some fun."
Well, the sad thing is he takes this huge corporation and says, "What can I do with it?"
Yeah, exactly.
"I can smack somebody."
And all his buddies are saying, "Hey, Stern really gave you guys the business.
Look what's happening to your radio division. You're losing ratings. You're losing revenue."
And then it comes out, you know, $9 billion in losses under Les's side of CBS.
There's going to be record lows in the radio division.
You know what? Well, Sue Howard Stern will take everyone's mind off of it.
But still, the bully is going to take the spider and pull his legs off one by one, the torturer.
He's going to torture me.
But the plan backfired. The lawsuit is ridiculous.
There wasn't an analyst on television yesterday who didn't say so, mocking him, laughing at him.
The articles in the financial papers are nothing short of hysterical.
Well, that's what I said. His reviews are now coming in and woohoo.
He never expected this. He never saw it coming because I told you he doesn't think more than one step ahead.
The truly great chess players. You know, when I lived in Roosevelt and I was getting my brains beaten in on a daily basis,
I retreated to my room and I began to teach myself how to play chess.
It's a stupid way to live. I sat in my room for years like a prisoner and read books and books about chess.
Is the caro-can defense good? Is it this? Is it that? Is it that? And what you begin to do is you train yourself to start to think six steps ahead of your opponent.
And it's a really good lesson for life. I recommend it because you've got to, when you run a company, think six steps ahead.
Where does this lead me? What's the end game? How do I check me? What's my plan?
And I realized quickly that chess, while I started to get very good at it, was a waste of my time. I'd rather do it with people.
I'd rather look at them and figure out the end game. And one thing I knew about Les, he swaggers when he comes into the room.
The same swagger David Lee Roth has. It's the swagger that says, "You know what? I can do anything. I'm invincible."
"I used to talk to 300,000 people at a concert, man. I know how to talk to people."
It's a swagger. There's no humility. A swagger. I've seen the swagger. I see it.
And I went to Les on a very human level and I said, "What are you doing? The suit has no merit. You're being led down the wrong path."
You're making a mistake. You're not thinking six moves ahead. You're not thinking two moves ahead. You're thinking you're invincible.
You've got the swagger. The network ratings are good, but you're not looking at the big picture. What are you doing?
And with your money, there are lawyers who will entertain this and play around and try to drag it out, even though it means nothing.
If he had to have spent his own money, wouldn't have happened. But I want to thank everyone, especially everyone who's now coming out of the woodwork.
Thank you.
But this is one of those things. Les doesn't even realize now what he's done to his own life.
Oh, no, he doesn't.
Because when he goes out now, he'll be the idiot who's suing Howard Stern.
I'm reading the Stern Fan Network. Someone wrote, and I don't agree with this at all. I thought it was actually pretty ridiculous.
But I said, someone wrote, "Does Les go to restaurants? Does he go out? Does he know that every Howard Stern fan works in these restaurants?
Works in these places?" He goes, "Does he know this? That people consider me their friend?"
But screw that. I'm talking about the merits of the lawsuit. I'm talking about what's your end game.
But I'm also talking about his friends. You know, when he goes to those cocktail parties and lords over the room, you know, that swagger that he walks in with?
Oh, the swagger. Everybody's going to be smirking while they shake your hand less because this idiot is the idiot who's suing Howard Stern.
I'll tell you how it gets. The guy, then I'll follow along with this. You're going to find it fascinating.
Every article says the lawsuit is groundless, they're bitter, and they're really doing the wrong thing.
So much so that Jack Thompson, the lawyer who went after me in Miami, and some claim is the reason that the FCC went after me and eventually I was thrown off the air.
So Jack, you know, Jack's certainly not someone who's a big fan of my career or my act or what I do for a living.

But Jack, not only, I'll play a clip from, he was on one of the TV channels last night, but in an article, he attacked CBS, which seeks in excess of $200 million in damages from Stern, Stern's agent Don Buckwald in Sirius.

The fact that Stern harmed CBS radio for promoting his move to Sirius for well over a year. Quote, "There are huge factual and legal problems with CBS's argument," as Thompson outlined today on the radio.
First, CBS, it goes on and on and on. Maybe I should just play this stuff rather than read it to you. I run the risk of boring you, but I'm so excited about these articles.
I'm so excited about an article that I would like to put this article up on the website so people can read it. It's called "Howard Stern's Chief Nemesis Defense Him Against CBS."
Here's another one. CBS can't be serious. I would like you to read. I think this one's already up on my website.
It talks about the ridiculousness of Les Moonves. How poorly thought out this is.
Judge Napolitano, who is the Fox News analyst and a judge, said that, "I probably now have a very good countersuit."
That's what I said yesterday. Maybe a countersuit is in order.
So let's visit.
This is harassment.
That's exactly what he said, and there's a very good claim for it. He says it's absolutely harassment.

They threatened Howard in the legal papers to go back and release all his salary figures from all the years he worked for them, which is nothing short of a threat to embarrass and cause Howard harm and damages.
And he said there is great legal precedent for this. I assure you, I am not backing down.
You know, when these kind of things come up, it makes you want to be a lawyer because you want to go after guys who want to do this to people.
The radio community is saying, "What did Les Moonves do to us?" Howard Stern was basically forgotten.
Today in the New York Post in page 6, it states, "Since Les Moonves did this, there's been a spike in sales of Sirius satellite radios, and that Les is a subscriber to Sirius satellite radios."
He's obviously losing it.
I cannot tell you how I warned this guy that this was ridiculous to leave me alone, and you know what?
Even be a friend and say, "Here's your dumb tapes. We're out of the Howard Stern business."
Why don't I let you go?
But he couldn't let me go, because Les - whatever his name is - Joel Hollander. How'd it have his ear?
Joel Hollander is a little bulldog that wants me punished for leaving. They're angry that I left.
You know, I'll tell you about Les Hollander, Joel Hollander. This guy is the head of the radio division, which I even questioned when it happened.
He appoints as one of the guys to run a division or something, his brother.
Out of all the radio professionals, he picks his brother who I'm not even sure has ever run a division before.
When you've got a guy who finally gets to be the president of the radio division, he appoints his brother that's the only guy he can find?
What are his qualifications to run a division? Doesn't anybody stop and question anything?
Doesn't anybody ask a question? I'll tell you who doesn't. The CEO of CBS Radio, Les Moonves, doesn't stop to ask questions.
He couldn't give a crap about the radio division, and I guarantee it's going down. They're going to lose tons of money and wait till you see the record losses.
He better start concentrating.
Well, I've said something about you that the first thing the guy you pick to run your division does is pick his brother.
If you want to realize you have no ability to pick.
And then what do you say to a guy who then - look at what they did. The radio division's going down the tubes.
And what does Les Moonves do? The head of the company? He changes the name to CBS Radio.
So now CBS Radio is a name known for losing record profits, which I believe they will. I haven't seen it yet.
That was his innovation. It was Infinity Broadcasting, and he decided to make it CBS Radio.
Yeah, he changed the name. Great. Now every article is CBS Radio going down the tubes. CBS, CBS, CBS, going down the tubes.
CBS Radio, record losses. Good move. Good business move.
Well, yesterday I told you the stockholders are probably having conniptions at this point.
Well, absolutely. Well, then what Gary just gave me, Yahoo, an analysis of the stocks.
Disney News Corp, which is Fox Time Warner via CBS, which is rather Les's little division.
Yeah.
Who's the lowest one on the chart here?
CBS.
Who's the one pointing downward?
CBS.
It's got to be a nightmare in the boardroom.
You don't want that to be pointing downward.
I don't know anything about the stock market. Honestly, I don't.
And I wouldn't propose that I could ever tell you how this would affect their stock.
But all I know is I'm looking at some whacked-out chart on Yahoo that doesn't look promising.
You know, down is bad.
Well, I'm not only just talking about what the stock will do, because the point is they will do anything to make that different.
They will do anything to make that arrow start pointing up.
And that means Les is putting himself in danger.
Les Moonves should have called me already and begged me to pay me.
That's what I think.
I think this guy made a classic blunder.
There is one article that I can read you later that says this could be the end of Les Moonves' career.
Absolutely.
That's how big a blunder this is.
And I'll read it to you.
I'm not making this stuff up.
This is exactly what I expected. The arrogance of this man to file frivolously is ridiculous.
And you ought to counter suit, because he might want to try to, you know, wait this out a few weeks and slide this off the table.
My lawyer has told me not to talk about my plans.
But my plans will be thorough. I will come at them with whatever I think is legally responsible.
But they shouldn't be let go of now.
No.
Oh, no.
No, no, no, no, no.
I'm having too much fun.
You know what?
I've got to tell you something.
Anyone who gets sued, you never should take it lightly.
And believe me, if you don't stay awake at night, you're a fool.
But I've got to tell you something.
My lawyers even say, don't sweat it.
Have fun with this.
Because they've put you right back in the newspapers.
But also they've put themselves in your hands.
Yes.
And you shouldn't let them go.
No, no, no, no, no.
There's no -- I told them there's no forgiveness here.
I don't like bullies.
And I'm not going to be bullied.
You know, they forgot I have one thing on my side.
I can go and speak out and be heard.
They forgot.
They forgot that they're going to be exposed.
Who sits in an office and can't get back at them.
I assure you at some point they'll try and shut me up.
You wait and see.
You watch.
Oh, yeah.
I'm not being shut up.
You wait and see.
I guarantee you that'll come out.
Let me tell you, this guy's in trouble.
Let's go listen to some tape together.
I'll make my point.
This is -- these are clips.
This one runs two minutes.
It's from O'Reilly last night, Bill O'Reilly's show.
I haven't heard it.
I asked them to take out the -- you know, take out relevant portions.
So CVS wants how much money from Stern?
They want $218 million in damages, not including punitive damages.
I mean, it's absurd.
It's an absurd amount of money.
Do you think that CVS has a case?
I don't think CVS has a case.
I think this is the grandest nuisance suit I've ever seen.
This is a vendetta gone wild.
Really?
Yes.
Basically, CVS had 14 months in which to get rid of Stern, replace Stern,
come up with a plan of how they were going to deal with the loss of Stern.
14 months.
They heard him every day on the radio talking about Stern.
It was no surprise to anyone that Stern was going to CVS.
He was talking about it every day on air.
Yeah, but if they got rid of him, then they would have to do all kinds of givebacks
on sponsors and advertising, and that would have hurt the CVS bottom line.
Didn't CVS have a right?
And you will assume that they'll come in at a deposition and say,
"Look, we told the guy not to do it.
He just defied us.
He violated this contract."
Now, what Stern's lawyers say, "Well, you could have fired him."
That's right.
But CVS --
That's not even what my lawyers are saying.
They could have hit a dump button.
That's right.
They could have given me a directive.
Because they hit it whenever they wanted to.
Yeah.
They never -- they didn't -- for, I think, a year, I didn't hear from them,
except to get calls from Joel saying, "Hey, you did a great job.
You're funny."
Two minutes delay.
Two minutes delay.
Did you ever see Tom come in and yell at me during commercials that,
"I better stop talking about this"?
Never.
Never, because I had an arrangement with Joel.
I was going to say, if we did that, we would have to do all these givebacks to sponsors,
and he was irresponsible.
Tough luck.
I mean, you can't have your cake and eat it, too.
If you have an employee that's violating a contract -- and in the suit,
they're saying that he violated the contract --
Right.
-- you had a right to fire him, with whatever damages or losses you incur from that --
Right.
Absolutely.
Fire me, then, and get all your damages if you think I damaged you.
That's what I said.
I made record profits for that company.
All right.
There was no damages.
That was the time to fire and sue.
They go, "Well, we didn't know --"
You had ground.
"We didn't know you were going to be paid by Sirius."
Yes, you did.
You can have them both ways.
You can't have them on air, promoting another station,
and say, "Well, if we get rid of them, we're going to lose money,"
and then have them off, and then sue them anyway, and say, "We're going to --"
Why do you think CBS is bothering you here?
I think this is pure vindictiveness.
Les Moomis, head of CBS, hates Howard Stern.
Howard Stern hates Les.
There's no love lost.
They've been very public about their mutual loathing,
and I think it's just -- it's taken on a whole other level.
It's that ridiculous.
He's a bully, and you want to know something?
He thought he was going to get it.
He said, "What the hell? It costs me nothing to bully Howard Stern."
He thought I was a target.
He thought I was Dan Rather's producers.
He thought I was the Ronald Reagan movie.
And by the way, he fully expects me to be quiet about it while he tortures me.
Oh, yeah.
He expects you to just dribble and quake and say,
"Please, how can I make this go away?"
He's going to file papers that call me a fraud and a crook,
and I'm supposed to be quiet about it?
Totally personal.
I think that's exactly what this is about.
Now, Napolitano, our legal guy, is going to be on next,
as a matter of fact, with another case.
He says that CBS is a legitimate case because there is damages,
that they can show damages to their organization,
and that, you know, you don't use --
It's not what I heard him say.
In fact, I'll play you what he said.
Somebody is good will to make money for somebody else.
No, this is Napolitano, but you're not buying it.
No, I'm not buying it.
Again, CBS had 14 months, and the best they could come up with,
the best that these geniuses could come up with,
was David Lee Ross, some washed-up rocker,
who is amusing to no one but his own mother,
and I just think they had ample time to deal with the loss of Howard Stern.
This is desperation.
This is CBS saying we're losing millions of dollars in advertising.
Some of their affiliates have lost 90% of their audience.
But CBS wants to cost Sirius some money.
You know who this is costing?
This is costing Viacom shareholders.
If I were a Viacom shareholder, I'd be pissed off.
This is a waste of money.
It's a frivolous suit.
All right.
Leah, thanks very much.
We appreciate it.
Leah gets it.
Wait, I'll play you Napolitano.
I don't think Bill O'Reilly had that right.
Napolitano says just the opposite.
Yeah, but the way he drones is what I'm talking about.
I'm like, "Oh, where am I?"
Napolitano says I should be countersuing.
Does CBS have a leg to stand on here?
How could --
This is Judge Napolitano.
They sue him for things he said on their radio station.
They made no attempts.
They could have taken him off the air.
Right.
I think Howard will have a very strong defense,
which is the whole industry knew that I was going to Sirius.
You knew it.
You didn't stop me from saying this.
You let me say it.
It's basically what lawyers call a waiver.
You gave up the right to challenge by keeping him in their employ
and letting him say what he was saying.
And so could one --
Letting me, encouraging me.
-- conclude that this lawsuit is a transparent attempt to slime Howard --
And to make him miserable by revealing to the world what he has earned from them.
Yes, one could make that conclusion.
And I hope that that's the counterclaim that Howard's lawyers file against CBS.
So you'd expect this thing to get tossed on day one.
I would.
Well, not on day one.
I think we're going to read a lot more about it,
and there'll be cross complaints and cross arguments and cross motions,
but tossed before it gets to a jury.
This is what happens when you become a billionaire or close to it.
Close to it.
All right.
Thanks a lot.
John Gibson hit it right on the head.
If I make some money, Les Moonves is green with envy.
And Joel Hollander -- because they -- you know --
Then you have to wonder about people who are looking around the world
and they begrudge another person doing well.
Yeah.
This is all this is.
And you know what?
Anything I've gotten is because I worked day in and day out.
Not them.
You see what they got on their radio stations now.
I delivered the goods for these guys.
I delivered the goods for these guys.
They didn't deliver anything to me.
And you were supposed to continue to be there, Lackey.
Yeah.
How dare you?
Break out and become your own man.
And not to contradict O'Reilly, but there is Judge Napolitano
saying that this case won't even last in a court.
There it is.
That's what he said.
I don't know what's wrong with Bill O'Reilly.
I don't know.
I don't know why he would say that.
Yeah.
What was he talking about?
It could be, sir.
Let's go on.
Let's see the wave of publicity for the great CEO, Les Moonves,
and with all of his great decisions.
His decisions are going to start coming to the forefront.
Yeah.
Apparently somebody's been protecting him up till now.
Well, he's been lucky.
He's flown under the radar because the network --
what he does best is pick television shows for you to watch.
That's all he is, in my opinion.
You're a bully and a torturer, and I'm not going to stand for it.
You want me up awake at night.
You want me with an ulcer.
Yeah, I mean, you must have been, you know,
thinking crazy to think you were going to come over here
and have a good time.
This is what the Radio Revolution is about.
When you listen to our channels, Howard 100, 1001,
and I know sometimes we get carried away with it,
but this is my revolution, and thank you for joining it.
But you have to realize that --
These guys are not to be screwed with.
That's their idea.
They run a-fief them.
Well, remember when they were putting out those ads
as we were leaving that, you know, Dick and Fart jokes --
Fart off.
Over.
Apparently they loved Dick and Fart jokes.
That's right.
Because they are so upset that they're gone.
Let's listen to more tapes.
To help assess the lawsuits, merits,
I'm joined now by criminal defense attorney Joe Sakapinac.
Joe, good evening.
Hi, Keith. How are you?
I'm not following the CBS argument here, are you?
Not really.
I mean, you know, I think this lawsuit can be dismissed
in the summary judgment stage,
which means before it's even litigated before.
Absolutely.
You know, the only thing that I worry about is if, you know,
if some judge wants to carry on with this for publicity reasons,
but my lawyer said to me,
"This is something -- this is something that will be --
because it's just ridiculous lawsuit."
He says, "I've never seen more bizarre claims."
There are depositions taken because, quite frankly,
you know, CBS here created these damages
by really amping up this -- the attention brought to it,
putting them on 60 Minutes, putting them on Letterman,
and now they want to collect off the damages
they seem to have created.
I was reminded the other --
If you want to sue someone,
Les should sue Joel Hollander.
Well, he doesn't have any leg to stand on there, either.
Yeah, I mean, Joel --
Because he kept him employed.
Joel allowed me to do everything.
And none of them.
Les -- they should sue themselves.
This is what the lawsuit is --
They don't have a leg to stand on.
This guy -- this guy, Les Moonva,
wanted me to lay in bed at night
and get an ulcer and room my weekend.
This is what a bully does.
You know, when you went to school, I certainly was bullied.
You lay in bed and go, "Oh, my God, I got to go back to school tomorrow
and face the world."
Yeah, you dread every day.
